content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 11, 1993


Tricia Hunter, R.N., M.N.
43-155 Portola, Sp. 50
P.O. Box 10353
Palm Desert, CA  92255

                                        Re:  Your request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-93-203

Dear Ms. Hunter:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice
concerning your duties under the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1

                              QUESTION

     Are funds that you receive for teaching continuing education
courses to registered nurses and other health care providers
prohibited honoraria under the Act?

                              CONCLUSION

     The funds you receive for teaching nursing courses, which
are related to your field of specialty and practice, are not
prohibited honoraria under the Act.

                              FACTS

     You are currently an appointed public official in the Office
of Statewide Health Planning and Development.  Independent of your
position with the Office of Statewide Health Planning and
Development, you have been asked to provide continuing education
for registered nurses and other health care providers.  The
compensation you would receive for providing this service would be
paid by the various entities requesting your services.


_____________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-203
Page 2


ANALYSIS

Under Section 89503(a):

     [N]o designated employee of a state agency,
     shall accept an honorarium from any source, if
     the . . . employee would be required to report
     the receipt of income or gifts from that
     source on his or her statement of economic
     interest.

     Because you are in a position where you make or participate
in making governmental decisions for the Office of Statewide
Health Planning and Development, you are a designated employee of
that agency and, therefore, all income you receive is to be
reported on your statement of economic interests.  (Section
87302(b).)  In addition, since you are a designated employee, you
are also subject to the honoraria ban of Section 89503.

     The question is whether the money you receive for providing
continuing education to registered nurses and other health
providers constitutes prohibited "honorarium."  As defined by the
Act,

          "Honorarium" means . . . any payment made in
          consideration for any speech given, article
          published, or attendance at any public or
          private conference, convention, meeting,
          social event, meal, or like gathering.

                                        Section 89502(b).

     However "honorarium" does not include:

          . . . [E]arned income for personal services
          which are customarily provided in connection
          with the individual's business or practice of
          a bona fide business, trade, or profession,
          such as teaching, practicing law, medicine,
          insurance, real estate, banking, or building
          contracting, unless the sole or predominant
          activity of the business, trade, or profession
          is making speeches. . . .

                                        Section 89502(c), emphasis added.

     Regulation 18932.2 defines the term "bona fide business" for
purposes of Sections 89502, 89503(b), and 89506(c)(3) in pertinent
part as follows:

Our File No. A-93-203
Page 3


     . . . [A]n individual is presumed to be
engaged in the bona fide profession of
teaching in any of the following
circumstances:

     The individual receives payment for
teaching a course, presented to assist in
maintenance or improvement of professional
skills or knowledge where the course provides
credit toward continuing education
requirements of the pertinent profession.

     Under this definition, the teaching of continuing education
courses would be considered a bona fide business, trade or
profession specifically exempted from the scope of the honoraria
prohibitions. (Section 89502(c).) Under the facts you have
presented, it appears that your teaching would fall within this
exception. Under these circumstances the money you receive for
your teaching these continuing education courses to registered
nurses and other health care professionals falls within the
exception of Section 89502(c) and is not a prohibited honorarium.2

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter
please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Jeff Marschner
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Lynda Doherty
                                            Staff Services Analyst
                                            Legal Division

:ld

Enclosures




_________________________
2     Please note, however, that the various entities that pay you
for your teaching services are potentially sources of income to
you. As such, you may have a conflict of interest if you make,
participate in making or otherwise use your official position to
influence a governmental decision that will foreseeably and
materially affect those agencies. (Section 87100, 87103.)

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are funds that you receive for teaching continuing education
courses to registered nurses and other health care providers
prohibited honoraria under the Act?

CONCLUSION: The funds you receive for teaching nursing courses,
which
are related to your field of specialty and practice,
are not
prohibited honoraria under the Act.
